title: Back-to-Back Testing
concept:
**Back-to-back testing** uses an alternative version of the system as a **pseudo-oracle** — a reference that provides expected outputs for comparison, even when no single authoritative expected output exists.

**How it works:**
1. Identify or build a pseudo-oracle: an alternative implementation of the system.
2. Run both the system under test and the pseudo-oracle on the same test inputs.
3. Compare outputs: significant discrepancies indicate potential defects in one or both systems.
4. Investigate discrepancies to determine which system is correct.

**Types of pseudo-oracle:**
- **Existing system**: The previous version of the model being updated. Differences are potential regressions.
- **Independently developed system**: A separate implementation using a different framework or algorithm, ideally developed independently to avoid shared defects.

**Key properties:**
- The pseudo-oracle only needs functional correctness (not non-functional requirements). It does not need to meet latency or resource requirements.
- Only test **inputs** are needed — no pre-defined expected outputs are required. This is its key advantage over conventional test case design.
- Particularly valuable when migrating to a new environment or framework.

**Comparison with A/B testing:**
| | Back-to-Back | A/B Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary purpose** | Detect defects | Compare performance |
| **When** | Development/migration | Production |
| **Traffic** | Offline/same inputs | Split production traffic |
| **Oracle** | Pseudo-oracle | No oracle needed |

**Note**: Back-to-back testing can partially address the test oracle problem but not completely — if both implementations share a design flaw, discrepancies will not be detected.
keyPoints: Back-to-back testing uses a pseudo-oracle (existing system or independent implementation) to compare outputs for identical inputs — detecting defects without pre-defined expected outputs.
The pseudo-oracle only needs functional accuracy, not non-functional compliance; only test inputs (not expected outputs) are needed to run back-to-back tests.
Back-to-back testing detects defects; A/B testing compares performance — they serve different purposes and back-to-back is typically an offline/development-time activity.

quiz.question: A team is migrating their recommendation engine from Framework A to Framework B. They want to verify the migrated model behaves identically to the original. Which technique is most appropriate?
options: A/B testing — route half the live production traffic to each version.
Back-to-back testing — run both the original Framework A model and the migrated Framework B model on the same offline test inputs, comparing their outputs for discrepancies.
Metamorphic testing — derive metamorphic relations to check output consistency.
Neuron coverage testing — verify the Framework B model achieves the same neuron coverage as Framework A.
quiz.explanation: Back-to-back testing is specifically designed for this scenario — framework migration. The original Framework A model serves as the pseudo-oracle. Both models are run on identical test inputs offline, and output discrepancies flag potential migration defects. A/B testing would expose real users to a potentially defective migrated model, which is riskier. Metamorphic testing checks relational properties but not exact equivalence.
